critic_raw:
Two methodological concerns arise in this design.

First, while metadata features are standardized using training-set statistics only, the text preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling) are fit on the entire dataset. The design explicitly states: 'all preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset.' For the vocabulary (5,000 tokens), fitting on the full dataset means the vocabulary includes words from validation and test-period patient feedback — a minor vocabulary leakage since clinical text vocabulary is relatively stable, but technically incorrect.

Second, for the final model evaluation, both models are retrained on combined training and validation data (months 1–18) before final test evaluation. The design specifies this correctly. However, the hyperparameters were selected to maximize validation ROC-AUC on a standalone validation set (months 13–18). When the model is retrained on the combined 18-month dataset, the training dynamics change (larger dataset, different data composition), potentially invalidating the hyperparameter selection made on the smaller training-only set. The design does not address how hyperparameter validity is maintained when the training set composition changes at final training time.
all_issues_raised: Text tokenizer/vocabulary fit on full dataset including validation and test — vocabulary leakage
Hyperparameters selected on standalone validation set, then model retrained on combined train+validation — hyperparameter validity not re-verified

round_transcripts.critic:
Two methodological concerns arise in this design.

First, while metadata features are standardized using training-set statistics only, the text preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling) are fit on the entire dataset. The design explicitly states: 'all preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset.' For the vocabulary (5,000 tokens), fitting on the full dataset means the vocabulary includes words from validation and test-period patient feedback — a minor vocabulary leakage since clinical text vocabulary is relatively stable, but technically incorrect.

Second, for the final model evaluation, both models are retrained on combined training and validation data (months 1–18) before final test evaluation. The design specifies this correctly. However, the hyperparameters were selected to maximize validation ROC-AUC on a standalone validation set (months 13–18). When the model is retrained on the combined 18-month dataset, the training dynamics change (larger dataset, different data composition), potentially invalidating the hyperparameter selection made on the smaller training-only set. The design does not address how hyperparameter validity is maintained when the training set composition changes at final training time.
round_transcripts.defender:
On vocabulary leakage: the vocabulary is capped at 5,000 tokens fit on the entire dataset. For clinical mental health feedback text, vocabulary stability over 24 months is high — patients use consistent terminology about medications, appointments, side effects. The leakage from including validation/test vocabulary is minimal: the practical impact is that the vocabulary is slightly larger and includes rare words from later months, which would otherwise be mapped to unknown tokens. This does not encode any outcome information.

On hyperparameter retraining: the concern about changing training dynamics is real. When model is retrained on 18 months of data versus 12, learning rate schedules, batch size effects, and early stopping criteria designed for the smaller dataset may not be optimal. However, this is a standard practice in NLP and clinical ML — select hyperparameters on the validation split, retrain on full train+validation with same hyperparameters for the final model. The implicit assumption is that hyperparameters are sufficiently robust to the training set size increase. A more rigorous design would include a brief sensitivity analysis.

round_transcripts.critic: Issue 1 is substantially defended for clinical text with stable vocabulary. Issue 2 is partially defended as standard practice but the validity concern remains — the design does not include the recommended sensitivity analysis. Issue 1 is largely resolved; issue 2 is partially mitigated.
round_transcripts.defender: Issue 1 is adequately defended for stable clinical vocabulary. Issue 2 is partially addressed by standard practice norms but a sensitivity analysis would strengthen the design.

all_issues_adjudicated: Hyperparameter validity when retraining on larger combined dataset — partially mitigated but sensitivity analysis absent
